Admission Date:  1994-01-22              Discharge Date:   2009-07-13

Date of Birth:  2010-01-29             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Zithromax / Neosporin Scar Solution / Latex / Pollen Extracts /
Cat Hair Std Extract / Bacitracin

Attending:Eugene
Chief Complaint:
Abdominal Aortic aneurysm

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1994-01-22: Resection and repair of abdominal aortic aneurysm
with 14-mm Dacron tube graft and distal aortic
endarterectomy.

1994-08-19: Exploratory laparotomy, sigmoid resection, and
end-colostomy.

1996-10-31: Right Thoracentesis


History of Present Illness:
The patient is an 82-year-old gentleman with past medical
history significant for hypertension, hypercholesterolemai, and
CAD s/p CABG 1990, who was recently found to have an abdominal
aortic aneurysm 3.5cm which enlarged to 4.6cm. He has previously
had a right femoral sarcoma removed and radiation therapy and
has a chronically swollen right leg.  CT angiogram showed a
heavily calcified
infrarenal aorta with 3 areas of distinct blowout, almost
looking like contained ruptures.  The largest one of this just
proximal to the renal arteries and approximately 4.6 cm in
diameter.  The aorta was heavily calcified both proximal and
distal to these areas of aneurysmal saccular degeneration
and more so at the aortic bifurcation.  Because of the unusual
saccular shape and the appearance that one of these may have
actually been an old chronic contained rupture, Dr. Brewer
recommended open repair, as he did not have an endovascular
option.

Past Medical History:
Past Medical History:
1.  Coronary artery disease status post myocardial infarction in
1989, which was treated with angioplasty at the Wilkes-barre Veterans Affairs Medical Center.
He
then underwent a four-vessel CABG at Eastern Long Island Hospital Mercy Harvard Hospital in
1990 by Dr. Amy Little.
2.  Atrial fibrillation.
3.  Hypertension.
4.  Elevated lipids.
5.  Asthma.

Past Surgical History:  Status post laparoscopic cholecystectomy
and status post right knee arthroscopy.


Social History:
Social History:  The patient is married He lives in 724 Miranda Rapids
North Samantha, NH 20909
and is a former philosophy professor Amy Hayes of education at
[**University/College **].  He quit
smoking a pipe back in 1984 and drinks approximately two wine
beverages with dinner each evening.


Family History:
No family history of aneurysms.

Abd: pos bs, obese, colostomy baf with stool

Ext:2+ femoral pulses B,  no edema neurovascular and sensory
exam grossly intact, nl strength There is no suspicious
adenopathy in the bilateral groins

CXR: Low lung volumes. Small right effusion. Increased fullness
of the right hilus and prominence of left basilar interstitial
markings may refelect mild institial pulmonary edema. Increased
density at the right base likely a summation of low lung
volumes, atelectasis, and mild edema. Stable right pleural
lipoma. Left PICC at high SVC. NG tip not seen below field of
view.

Findings

This study was compared to the prior study of 1996-07-17.
LEFT ATRIUM: Dilated LA.

RIGHT ATRIUM/INTERATRIAL SEPTUM: Markedly dilated RA. Lipomatous
hypertrophy of the interatrial septum.

LEFT VENTRICLE: Overall normal LVEF (>55%). No resting LVOT
gradient.

RIGHT VENTRICLE: Normal RV chamber size and free wall motion.
Paradoxic septal motion consistent with prior cardiac surgery.

AORTIC VALVE: Mildly thickened aortic valve leaflets. No AS. No
AR.

MITRAL VALVE: Mildly thickened mitral valve leaflets. Mild (1+)
MR.

TRICUSPID VALVE: Mildly thickened tricuspid valve leaflets. Mild
[1+] TR. Moderate PA systolic hypertension.

PULMONIC VALVE/PULMONARY ARTERY: Pulmonic valve not well seen.

PERICARDIUM: No pericardial effusion.

GENERAL COMMENTS: Suboptimal image quality - bandages,
defibrillator pads or electrodes. Suboptimal image quality -
body habitus.
Conclusions  The left atrium is dilated. The right atrium is
markedly dilated. Overall left ventricular systolic function is
normal (LVEF>55%). No regional wall motion abnormalitu seen in
suboptimal views.Right ventricular chamber size and free wall
motion are normal. The aortic valve leaflets are mildly
thickened (?#). There is no aortic valve stenosis. No aortic
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. The tricuspid
valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is moderate pulmonary
artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial effusion.



Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Miller was admitted on 1994-01-22 for same day elective open AAA
repair. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to
the CVICU for monitoring. Overnight, he was hypotensive
requiring vasopressors and febrile to 101.7. Blood,sputum, and
urine cultures were sent and he was started on empiric
Vancomycin, ciprofloxacin and Flagyl. He remained intubated
overnight.

On POD2, the epidural catheter was removed. He continued to have
febrile episodes and remained on broad spectrum antibiotics
pending culture results. On exam, his abdomen was mildly tender
to palpation and distended. All cultures were negative on POD4
but he continued to have fevers. Transplant surgery was
consulted for a potential abdominal pathology, and a CT scan was
obtained demonstrating pneumatosis of the sigmoid colon. He was
taken to the operating 1994-08-19 for suspicion of bowel
ischemia. He underwent exploratory laparotomy that demonstrated
necrotic sigmoid colon, which was resected with creation of an
end-colostomy. He required intermittent pressors during the
case, and was taken intubated to CVICU on Neo-Synephrine drip.

On POD 6 and 1 from the AAA repair and ex-lap, respectively, he
was intubated on one pressor in the ICU, continuing to have low
grade fevers and a mildly elevated white count. He was volume
overloaded and was maintained on a Lasix drip for adequate
diuresis. He was extubated on POD3 from the ex-lap, and
maintained oxygen saturation on nasal cannula. He had return of
bowel function through the ostomy on POD5 from the ex-lap. He
was continued on broad spectrum antibiotics pending culture
results, and remained afebrile. He was maintained on Lasix drip
for further diuresis.

On POD 11(from AAA repair)/6(from ex-lap) he experienced
increasing respiratory distress and required re-intubation.
Chest xray demonstrated a large pleural effusion. He underwent
right thoracentesis to drain 550cc pleural fluid on 1996-10-31.
Diuresis was continued with Lasix drip. Cultures from
bronchoalveolar lavage were growing serratia, and he was started
on cefepime. On 04-26 sedation was weaned off to assess mental
status; he was interactive and following commands, tolerating
tube feeds at goal, with functioning ostomy. He was successfully
extubated 2001-11-05 and remained stable on nasal cannula. While on
the Lasix drip, pt experienced an increase in his creatinine to
2.0, once off the Lasix drip pt, creatinine has normalized.

10-21 - 02-23,  pt remained in the CVICU. During this time frame the
Lasix drip was stopped, because of his creatinine. Again this
has normalized, pt also had increase in sodium from around 147 -
154. He was treated with free water. On DC sodium has
normalized. Pt received TF during this period of time. When
bowel function returned, he was slowly started on sips. It was
noticed that he had difficulty swallowing. Pt did receive speech
and swallow, recommended Ground foods; Thin liquids Supplement
for dysphagia; Calorie counts were done pt PO intake was
minimal. They recommended TF, These are now cycled from 6am to 6
pm. These are to continue until PO intake is improved. Pt then
transferred to the floor.

08-10 - 11-22, PT and case management was then consulted for rehab
placement. during this time frame was nutritional support, with
TF, pt worked . Pt stable for rehab on 11-22. Foley remains in
place, this can be taken out at your discretion. Pt is taking
PO.

20. Lasix 20 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.
21. lisinopril 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day.

Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker

Discharge Instructions:
Division of Vascular and Endovascular Surgery

Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) Surgery Discharge Instructions

What to expect when you go home:
1. It is normal to feel weak and tired, this will last for 07-10
weeks
??????	You should get up out of bed every day and gradually increase
your activity each day
??????	You may walk and you may go up and down stairs
??????	Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too
much right away!

2. It is normal to have incisional and leg swelling:
??????	Wear loose fitting pants/clothing (this will be less
irritating to incision)
??????	Elevate your legs above the level of your heart (use 05-01
pillows or a recliner) every 2-3 hours throughout the day and at
night
??????	Avoid prolonged periods of standing or sitting without your
legs elevated

3. It is normal to have a decreased appetite, your appetite will
return with time
??????	You will probably lose your taste for food and lose some
weight
??????	Eat small frequent meals
??????	It is important to eat nutritious food options (high fiber,
lean meats, vegetables/fruits, low fat, low cholesterol) to
maintain your strength and assist in wound healing
??????	To avoid constipation: eat a high fiber diet and use stool
softener while taking pain medication

What activities you can and cannot do:
??????	No driving until post-op visit and you are no longer taking
pain medications
??????	You should get up every day, get dressed and walk, gradually
increasing your activity
??????	You may up and down stairs, go outside and/or ride in a car
??????	Increase your activities as you can tolerate- do not do too
much right away!
??????	No heavy lifting, pushing or pulling (greater than 5 pounds)
until your post op visit
??????	You may shower (let the soapy water run over incision, rinse
and pat dry)
??????	Your incision may be left uncovered, unless you have small
amounts of drainage from the wound, then place a dry dressing
over the area that is draining, as needed
??????	Take all the medications you were taking before surgery,
unless otherwise directed
??????	Take one full strength (325mg) enteric coated aspirin daily,
unless otherwise directed
??????	Call and schedule an appointment to be seen in 2 weeks for
staple/suture removal

What to report to office:
??????	Redness that extends away from your incision
??????	A sudden increase in pain that is not controlled with pain
medication
??????	A sudden change in the ability to move or use your leg or the
ability to feel your leg
??????	Temperature greater than 101.5F for 24 hours
??????	Bleeding from incision
??????	New or increased drainage from incision or white, yellow or
green drainage from incisions
